claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions.

**First, let's start with information about your child:**

**How old is your child?** Specifically, are they:
- Under 16 years old?
- Between 16-19 years old?
- 20 or over?

This is important because eligibility depends on the child's age and, if they're 16-19, whether they're in approved education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. Actually, I have two children I'd like to ask about. 

Alex is 14 years old, and Blake is 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for both Alex (14 years old) and Blake (19 years old).

Let me work through each child's situation:

**Starting with Alex (14 years old):**

Since Alex is under 16, they would generally qualify as a "qualifying young person" for Child Benefit purposes. However, I need to check one important thing:

**Is Alex working full-time?** (Full-time work is typically defined as 24 hours or more per week in paid employment that is not approved training.)

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Please let me know about Alex's work situation, and then we'll move on to Blake's situation (where their age of 19 means we'll need to check their education or training status).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, Alex is in school and not working. 

**Next question about Alex:** Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Most children in school don't receive these benefits, but I need to confirm this to complete the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! 

**One more question about Alex:** Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program? 

(Since Alex is 14 and in school, this is unlikely, but I need to check this as certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Alex is looking good so far for eligibility. 

Now I need to check some information about **you as the claimant** (which will apply to both children):

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?** Specifically, do any of these apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and are working/looking for work/studying with sufficient resources, OR
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Most people simply normally live in the UK. Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect!

**Now, regarding your responsibility for the children:** Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you contribute financially to their upkeep?

This is to confirm you're responsible for them - which typically means either:
- You live with them, OR
- You contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit toward their care (e.g., through maintenance payments, providing food, clothes, housing, etc.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but Blake does live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me understand the situation for each child:

**For Blake (19 years old):** Since Blake lives with you, that establishes your responsibility for them.

**For Alex (14 years old):** Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask: **Do you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep?** 

This could include:
- Maintenance payments (under a court order or agreement)
- Regular contributions for food, clothes, pocket money, housing
- Other financial support that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount

Do you provide financial support for Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is important for the assessment.

**For Alex (14 years old):** Since you don't live with Alex and you're not contributing financially to their upkeep, you would not meet the "responsibility" requirement for Alex. This means **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**For Blake (19 years old):** Blake lives with you, so you do have responsibility for them. However, since Blake is 19 years old, I need to check their education or training status.

Let me navigate to check Blake's situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now for Blake (19 years old):** Since Blake is between 16-19, their eligibility depends on their education or training status.

**Is Blake currently:**
1. **In approved education or training?** (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below, or other approved courses), OR
2. **Has Blake recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)? (There may be an extension period), OR
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave?

Which situation applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is still in education - studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! A-levels are approved education. Now I need to check a few more details about Blake's education:

**1. Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study - most A-level courses are full-time)

**2. Is the course funded by an employer or linked to a future job with an employer?** (Most A-level courses at school or college are not employer-funded)

Can you confirm these details about Blake's A-level studies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is studying full-time at sixth form college. It's not funded by an employer - just regular A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Blake's A-level course at sixth form college is approved education.

**Next question about Blake:** Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week in paid employment (or not working at all)?

(Students can work part-time and still qualify, but working 24 or more hours per week would affect eligibility.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work at all - just focused on studying.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know Blake is focused on studies.

**Next question about Blake:** Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**One more question about Blake:** Is Blake in any type of apprenticeship program, or are they just doing regular A-levels?

(You mentioned they're at sixth form college doing A-levels, so I expect they're not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in an apprenticeship - just doing regular A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now continuing with Blake's assessment - we've already confirmed you live in the UK (residency requirement ✓), so let me move forward:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed Blake lives with you (responsibility requirement ✓), so let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about Blake:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or planning to claim for them?

(For example, Blake's other parent or another person responsible for them?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake's other parent is already claiming for them and they live with Blake as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This is a crucial detail.

**For Blake:** Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and they live with Blake, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at the same time.

**Here's what this means:**
- If you and Blake's other parent both have responsibility for Blake (both living with them), you would need to decide between yourselves who should claim
- If you both want to claim and can't agree, HMRC would need to make the decision based on who has primary responsibility

**Let me ask:** Do you and Blake's other parent agree on who should be claiming Child Benefit for Blake? Or is this something you're trying to sort out?

Also, just to be clear about the overall situation:
- **For Alex (14):** You mentioned Alex doesn't live with you and you don't contribute financially to their care, so you wouldn't be eligible to claim for Alex
- **For Blake (19):** Blake's other parent is already claiming, and Blake lives with both of you

Are you looking to potentially transfer the claim for Blake to yourself, or are you just checking your eligibility options?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we've agreed that Blake's other parent should be the one claiming - I'm just trying to understand if I could claim if things were different.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that! That's very helpful to understand your situation.

Let me provide you with a complete assessment based on everything we've discussed:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Summary**

Based on our conversation, here's your complete eligibility assessment:

---

### **For Alex (14 years old): NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

**Why:** While Alex meets the basic age requirement (under 16), you do not meet the **responsibility requirement**. To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of Child Benefit)

Since Alex doesn't live with you and you're not making financial contributions to their care, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**If things were different:** If you started paying maintenance or contributing to Alex's upkeep (equal to or exceeding the Child Benefit amount), you could potentially become eligible to claim.

---

### **For Blake (19 years old): NOT CURRENTLY ELIGIBLE** ❌ 

**Why:** This is a different situation - Blake actually **meets all the eligibility requirements**:
- ✓ Age 16-19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels)
- ✓ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✓ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✓ You live in the UK (residency requirement met)
- ✓ Blake lives with you (responsibility requirement met)

**However**, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming and you've both agreed they should continue, you cannot claim right now.

**If things were different:** If circumstances changed and you and Blake's other parent agreed to transfer the claim to you, you would meet all the requirements and could claim Child Benefit for Blake. The transfer would need to be arranged with HMRC.

---

### **Important Note:**
Blake's eligibility will continue as long as they remain in approved full-time education until they turn 20, or until the end of the school term after they turn 19 (whichever comes first). Once Blake finishes their A-levels or turns 20, Child Benefit will stop.

---

**Do you have any questions about this assessment or what would need to change for you to become eligible?**

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). Alex is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship, which are all positive factors. You meet the UK residency requirements. However, you do not meet the 'responsibility' requirement for Alex because: (1) Alex does not live with you, AND (2) you do not contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (no maintenance payments or financial support). The responsibility requirement states you must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit toward their care. Since neither condition is met, you are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), which qualifies them as a 'qualifying young person' for Child Benefit purposes. Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours/week), not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - all positive factors. You meet the UK residency requirements, and you meet the responsibility requirement as Blake lives with you. However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you have both agreed that the other parent should continue to be the claimant. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Therefore, you are NOT currently eligible to claim for Blake. However, if circumstances changed and you and the other parent agreed to transfer the claim to you, you would meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). For Alex, the claimant fails the responsibility requirement as they neither live with Alex nor contribute financially to their upkeep. For Blake, while the claimant meets all other eligibility criteria (Blake is in approved education, lives with the claimant, etc.), Blake's other parent is already claiming and both parents have agreed this arrangement should continue. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so the claimant cannot currently claim for Blake. The claimant could potentially claim for Blake in the future if circumstances changed and both parents agreed to transfer the claim.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). Alex is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship, which are all positive factors. You meet the UK residency requirements. However, you do not meet the 'responsibility' requirement for Alex because: (1) Alex does not live with you, AND (2) you do not contribute financially to Alex's upkeep (no maintenance payments or financial support). The responsibility requirement states you must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit toward their care. Since neither condition is met, you are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), which qualifies them as a 'qualifying young person' for Child Benefit purposes. Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours/week), not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - all positive factors. You meet the UK residency requirements, and you meet the responsibility requirement as Blake lives with you. However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you have both agreed that the other parent should continue to be the claimant. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Therefore, you are NOT currently eligible to claim for Blake. However, if circumstances changed and you and the other parent agreed to transfer the claim to you, you would meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). For Alex, the claimant fails the responsibility requirement as they neither live with Alex nor contribute financially to their upkeep. For Blake, while the claimant meets all other eligibility criteria (Blake is in approved education, lives with the claimant, etc.), Blake's other parent is already claiming and both parents have agreed this arrangement should continue. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so the claimant cannot currently claim for Blake. The claimant could potentially claim for Blake in the future if circumstances changed and both parents agreed to transfer the claim.
